title: Generics & Wrapper Classes
description: Decode Java's type parameter syntax, autoboxing traps, and wrapper class utilities — concepts that appear in almost every exam code snippet involving collections and type conversion.

sections.content: 🧬 Why Generics & Wrappers Are Exam-Critical

CSE110 exams test generics in two ways: **reading** code with type parameters (e.g., `ArrayList<T>`, `Pair<K,V>`) and **writing** generic methods or classes. Wrapper classes are tested through autoboxing/unboxing traps, conversion methods (`Integer.parseInt`, `String.valueOf`), and comparisons (`==` vs `.equals()` on Integer objects). Understanding both is essential for the Collections chapter and for writing flexible, reusable code.
📦 Wrapper Classes — Primitives as Objects

Java's primitive types (`int`, `double`, `char`, etc.) are **not** objects and cannot be used with generics or collections. Each primitive has a corresponding **wrapper class** in `java.lang` that boxes the primitive into an object. Collections like `ArrayList<int>` are illegal — you must use `ArrayList<Integer>`.
🔄 Autoboxing and Unboxing

**Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion of a primitive to its wrapper class. **Unboxing** is the reverse. Java performs these conversions automatically in most contexts, but the process has subtle traps that appear frequently in exam predict-output questions.

sections.title: Autoboxing and Unboxing in Action

sections.label: Integer == Comparison Trap — Most Tested Autoboxing Bug
sections.content: **Never** compare `Integer` objects with `==` for values outside [-128, 127]. Java caches Integer objects in this range, so `Integer a = 100; Integer b = 100; a == b` is `true`. But `Integer a = 200; Integer b = 200; a == b` is `false` because two different objects are created. **Always use `.equals()`** for Integer comparison. This is the #1 autoboxing mistake in CSE110 exams.

sections.title: Integer == vs .equals() — The Cache Trap

sections.content: 🔧 Wrapper Class Utility Methods
Wrapper classes provide essential static utility methods for parsing, converting, and inspecting values. These appear constantly in exam code — you must recognize what each returns.

sections.label: parseInt vs valueOf — Different Return Types
sections.content: `Integer.parseInt("5")` returns a **primitive** `int`. `Integer.valueOf("5")` returns an **Integer object**. Both work in most contexts via autoboxing, but the distinction matters when the return type is explicitly tested, or when you need to avoid potential `NullPointerException` from unboxing.
🧩 Introduction to Generics

**Generics** allow classes, interfaces, and methods to operate on objects of various types while providing **compile-time type safety**. Before generics (pre-Java 5), collections stored `Object` and required explicit casting — leading to runtime `ClassCastException`. Generics eliminate this by checking types at compile time.

sections.title: Why Generics Exist — Pre-Generics vs Generics

sections.content: 🏗️ Generic Classes

You can define your own generic class by placing a **type parameter** in angle brackets after the class name: `class Box<T>`. `T` is a convention (Type), but you can use any letter. Common conventions: `T` (Type), `E` (Element), `K` (Key), `V` (Value), `N` (Number).

sections.title: Generic Class — Box<T> and Pair<K,V>

sections.content: ⚙️ Generic Methods

A **generic method** declares its own type parameter, independent of the class's type parameter. The type parameter appears before the return type: `public static <T> T method(T arg)`. Generic methods can be in non-generic classes.

sections.title: Generic Methods — Swap and Print

sections.content: 🚧 Bounded Type Parameters

Use `<T extends SomeClass>` to restrict what types can be passed as `T`. This lets you call methods of `SomeClass` on `T` objects inside the generic code. `extends` here means **extends or implements** (works for both classes and interfaces). The most common bound in exams is `<T extends Comparable<T>>`.

sections.title: Bounded Generics and Wildcards

sections.content: ⚠️ Generics Limitations — What You CANNOT Do

Generics have restrictions due to **type erasure** — Java removes generic type information at runtime, replacing all `T` with `Object` (or the bound type). This causes several limitations that exams love to test.

sections.label: Type Erasure in Exam Questions
sections.content: When exam code shows `List list = new ArrayList()` (no type parameter), this is a **raw type**. Raw types suppress all generic type checking. The compiler will show an **unchecked warning** (not an error). Operations on raw types may cause runtime `ClassCastException`. In exams, always identify raw types as a code quality issue even if the code compiles.
🏋️ Interactive Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Autoboxing and Integer Cache
sections.explanation: Line 1: `a == b` → true because 100 is within [-128, 127] cache range; same object reference. Line 2: `a.equals(b)` → true always (compares values). Line 3: `c == d` → false because 200 is outside the cache range; two distinct Integer objects are created by autoboxing. Line 4: `c.equals(d)` → true (value comparison). Line 5: `a + c` triggers unboxing of both to int (100 + 200 = 300); result is an int printed as 300.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Generic Box Class Trace
sections.explanation: Line 1: `sb.get()` returns `"hello"` (a String), `.toUpperCase()` returns `"HELLO"`. Line 2: `ib.get()` returns `10` (Integer, unboxed to int), `* 2 = 20`, autoboxed back to Integer, stored via `set`. `ib.get()` now returns 20. Line 3: `db.get()` returns `3.14` (Double, unboxed), `3.14 > 3.0` → `true`.

sections.title: Exercise 3: NullPointerException from Unboxing null
sections.error: Line 7 (`int val = map.get("c")`): Runtime NullPointerException. `map.get("c")` returns `null` because the key "c" does not exist. Java then attempts to **unbox** `null` into an `int`, which throws a NullPointerException at runtime.
sections.explanation: When `HashMap.get()` returns `null` for a missing key, and you assign it to a primitive `int`, Java tries to call `null.intValue()` — which crashes. The fix: use `getOrDefault(key, defaultValue)` which returns the default instead of null. Alternatively, assign to `Integer` (not `int`) and null-check before use. This pattern is extremely common in exam code analysis questions.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Wrapper Utility Methods
sections.explanation: `parseInt("255")` → int 255; `255+1=256`. `toBinaryString(255)` → "11111111" (8 ones = 255 in binary). `Integer.MAX_VALUE == 2147483647` → true (this is the maximum int value). `Character.isDigit('7')` → true. `Character.toUpperCase('m')` → 'M' (printed as M). `String.valueOf(3.14)` → "3.14" (double to String).

sections.title: Exercise 5: Generic Stack Implementation
sections.approach: Use `ArrayList<T>` as backing storage. The top of the stack is the last element of the ArrayList (index size-1). push = add to end (O(1)), pop = remove from end (O(1)), peek = get from end without removing (O(1)). Generic class declaration allows reuse with any type.
sections.explanation: The generic parameter `<T>` lets the same Stack class work for Integer, String, or any reference type. `ArrayList<T>` as backing store gives O(1) amortized push and pop (from the end). The `remove(data.size()-1)` idiom removes and returns the last element, which is the stack top. The check for empty before pop/peek prevents ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException by throwing a more descriptive RuntimeException. Notice how `push(1)` autoboxes int 1 to Integer automatically.

sections.content: 📝 Quick Reference Summary

items: **Wrapper classes**: `int`→`Integer`, `double`→`Double`, `char`→`Character`, `boolean`→`Boolean` — required for generics and collections
**Autoboxing**: automatic primitive→wrapper conversion (`Integer x = 5`); **unboxing**: wrapper→primitive (`int n = integerObj`)
**Null unboxing = NullPointerException**: `int n = map.get("missing")` crashes if get returns null — use `getOrDefault` or assign to wrapper type
**Integer cache trap**: `Integer` objects are cached for values -128 to 127; `==` returns `true` in range but `false` outside — always use `.equals()` for Integer comparison
**Generic class**: `class Box<T> { T val; }` — T replaced by actual type at compile time
**Generic method**: `public static <T> T method(T arg)` — type parameter before return type
**Bounded type**: `<T extends Comparable<T>>` — restricts T to types that implement Comparable
**Wildcard**: `List<?>` accepts any List type; `List<? extends Number>` accepts List of Number or subclasses
**Type erasure**: generic types are removed at runtime — cannot do `new T()`, `new T[]`, or `instanceof T`
**Key parsing methods**: `Integer.parseInt(str)` → int, `Double.parseDouble(str)` → double, `String.valueOf(val)` → String, `Integer.toBinaryString(n)` → binary string
